which leads us to a big principle and that is what I call the healthy paranoia factor. That's one of my favorite quotes. Just because you're paranoid it doesn't mean they're not out to get you. Healthy paranoia. You know Napoleon Hill in the work that went into thinking Row rich found 17. The characteristics held in common of all the successful people and one of them was he identified as accurate thinking and that is dealing again dealing with reality in a positive way but not ignoring reality trying to be a positive thinker and so healthy paranoia says you deal with reality. I'm going to give you one example that nobody likes to deal with. It came out of some experience I had I don't know ten years ago now. For several years I worked with the number one expert in retail theft control. Supermarket convenience store and drug store businesses and there's a there's a belief amongst the public and there's a belief in the industry that their number one theft problem is shoplifting. You know nasty customers who come in there and steal stuff. That's not their problem. 80% of their theft is internal not external and 80% of their theft is divided pretty evenly between their employees and the people who deliver stuff to the stores. They the delivery people steal the stuff back that they deliver after it's been inventoried in and and they also steal other stuff while they're there. So like the guys that deliver the cigarettes will have a hollowed out cigarette carton that they can fit a bunch of cameras in and so they'll steal cameras. The beer guys the best story I can tell you this beer this little convenience store was on record is selling hundreds and hundreds of cases of beer a week but there was no money. The owner finally had the place staked out and here goes the beer guy and he wheels in one thing of beer crates and yells to the guy behind a register checking him in five cases wheels out the back door wheels around the front wheels the same five cases and yells five cases does this five times checks in 25 cases he leaves five he's now got 20 cases that he can sell for cash to his buddies or to a friend that runs a bar who'll pay him cash under the table etc etc so delivery man theft is half the problem and then employee theft is the other half and and here's what I learned from that business and it is absolutely positively research supported true but it's something that nobody likes to deal with and that is that there are there are five percent basically five percent of the adult population will not steal that's losing that that's I'm gone that's not helping me I now have no sound one two okay there's five percent of the people who will not steal they are wired so they can't they will starve before they'll steal a piece of bread out of a dumpster without going and asking if they can have it there's five percent of the people who steal whether it's necessary or not they are compulsive they must lie cheat steel all the time and everything they did what for no benefit whatsoever they will lie cheat and steal they just cannot tell the truth they when they bury him they screw him in the ground in between in between the other ninety percent all of us probably most of us since the other ninety percent which includes everybody who works for you and every vendor who sells to you will steal given three circumstances here they are real quick just this is not the main point but here they are they will steal if they believe they can go undetected they have a perceived need and by the way if you put 500 millionaires in the room and ask him how many need more money 499 hands go up and they can rationalize the behavior now here's the trick to theft control you can't control two or three you can't you can't you can't impact you cannot control their ability to rationalize it you cannot control no matter how much you pay him you cannot control the need the only thing you can possibly control is whether or not they think they can go undetected the illustration seminar will use as if a mama just baked 24 new cookies and she and Johnny are the only ones home and she's gonna run down to the story get milk if she doesn't want to meet any of the cookies here's the all here's what she must do she's got to take Johnny into the cookies and she say there's 24 cookies there see them and you and I are the only ones in the house and I'm leaving and the first thing I'm doing when I come back in the door is count the cookies if there's anything less than 24 there you're it then Johnny no we'll be but under any other circumstances she's gonna come home and find cookies gone and Johnny's not gonna know nothing and so you the same things true with employees the same things true with vendors now that's that's fact but what you get from everybody is all of the denial stuff well my employees would never steal from me we're one big family we love each other my employees would never steal from me because I pay them more than the and all of the other reasons that everybody wants to ignore this information because it's painful and unpleasant information but if you want to run a real sound business then you indulge in healthy paranoia instead of denial and you implement count the cookie systems for everything you have in the place it was not particularly important to me at the time but when I had a fairly large company and a bunch of employees which Steve will remember one time we figured out that at the rate we were using toilet paper now Steve was a bigger boy then but but still at the rate we were at the rate where we were using toilet paper we must have zero productivity because everybody's got to be in the John all day long obviously the toilet paper is walking out the back door going home that's where the toilet paper is going now at the time I didn't I had bigger fish to fry but it's true in every business and so there's all of these little unpleasant pieces of information that are true and factual and real that fall under the category of healthy paranoia in the speaking business healthy paranoia rule number one is have anybody work for you odds are the ad agency business the same they will leave you at some point in time with your client file under their arm and will pursue all of your clients it's a given and so now you can try and control that or you cannot try and control that but you got it but you can't say to yourself well it's not going to happen with him because he grew up with my brother and they went to it's not going to happen with her because she has no ambition she'll never leave me it's not going to know it will and so the smart business person functions with the bad news not the good news there's three things that I say you never delegate number one is the checkbook watch the money empty numbers of Hollywood personalities and celebrities and athletes and business people can all tell you horror stories I will tell you one quick one friend some years ago has a company they're doing millions and millions of dollars a month in business and they're getting further and further behind and paying their bills he can't figure it out but like many of us he's so busy running doing selling that he doesn't have time to really figure out this problem his lifelong friend who is running the money has at peak 23 ghost companies that all have vendor sounding names to which he is diverting all of the money and when finally the bubble bursts and the vendors won't the real vendors won't deliver anything anymore and the company comes to a grinding halt lifelong friend of 30 years is gone along with about 11 million dollars happens in small offices too on a smaller scale you got to watch the checkbook if you're not going to sign every check you ought to at least have your nose in it once a month look at the stubs see who's been paid how much see if you recognize some names that are unfamiliar to you amounts that are weird don't delegate the checkbook don't delegate the marketing it's the highest payoff activity there is you cannot make more money doing anything else than doing the marketing and so it just drives us marketing guys crazy when we hear from a client well I'd like to do all that stuff but I don't have time to do that well what else are you doing that's the business well see no the business is putting stuff on the shelves and going out and running errands every day from noon to two and whatever else now the business is marketing you don't delegate that and you don't delegate the legal as ugly as that is you ought to know what they're doing don't just the worst thing you can ever hear from an attorney is don't worry I have it under control not good not good at all in dealing with your experts here's a biggie called the core competency factor a lot of people get themselves in trouble this way here's here's the deal the temptation is when you find somebody who's really smart has legitimate experience and is really helpful to you in one area to suddenly give them credit for being equally smart about a whole bunch of other stuff which they are not very few people are good advisors about everything I try not to talk to you about stuff I don't know anything about but there are a lot of people I know who are cheerfully happy to talk to customers clients and audiences about what they know a lot about and what they don't know a lot about and with no difference between the two there's a guy I'm not going to say his name because you would know him too but I noticed that last year he was speaking at two high price seminars and one of the things he was talking about was how to do infomercials now this is a direct response guy with great qualifications in his own area of expertise however in seven years he's only produced four shows and all four of them were instant deadly fatal flops he has no more idea how to do an effective infomercial than I do you know about how to cook dinner I mean he'll never pull off a successful infomercial and I'd starve if I had to cook and I got no business doing a cooking class and he's got no business talking about infomercials but because he's really smart over here people will listen to him about that too thinking he knows something he doesn't and so as you deal with people who help you it's important to get a grip on what I call their core competency factor what they do really really well what they have legitimate track records connected to what you should be listening to them about and then the other stuff that they talked to you about you either ignore it entirely or you know listen to be polite but pretty much you don't give them much credit and you got to sort out the two I'll just throw this slide up there by the way everybody's bugging me for the slides I'll talk to you about that in a second I'm not gonna spend any time on this but I am I'll put it up there because a lot of people mess this up nob said nob said moving on to money for a second here's a cartoon I love looking at I'll read you the caption can't read the caption the guys got the eye shade on sees a little count it's up to you now Miller the only thing he can save us is an accounting breakthrough I thought that was a great cartoon brings us to a couple of money things here's here's here's here's here's the first one a lot of people mess this up I did once for a while has the owner your job is to get money out not to put money in that thing's supposed to be spinning off money not sucking it up and you do not want to leave all of your money in your company not a good thing to do which which brings us to another issue actually two issues there's a whole lot of people in business who do not really understand the difference between gross and net and then the difference can be fit I had an early mentor who basically I was in business with for five years and here with his business philosophy his business philosophy was if there's enough gross there's got to be some net around here somewhere let me let me tell you that ain't necessarily so and some businesses by the way can go for long periods of time doing huge grosses with no net before it catches up to them and kills them but it inevitably kills them and really his attitude was the solution to everything is more gross and that is the solution actually to very few things most problems that are in that exist in a business multiply when you multiply the gross they don't go away when you multiply the gross they just get bigger themselves and feed on the problem there's a whole issue of comparing numbers you know people want to compare the gross sales numbers in the speaking business everybody wants to compare the number of dates they do every year in direct response where everybody wants to compare response percentages all none of those numbers count for nothing net is the only number that counts and it is not uncommon to see individuals or companies getting awards and enormous recognition one year and having to be out of business the next because that number was not a good number and you'd be amazed how many clients don't know or they know here's what a lot of clients know they know the net from the whole business but they cannot tell you next when you bust up the business they cannot tell you which is the most profitable thing they do which is the least profitable thing they do they don't know they can't tell you you got to know and you got to continually be killing off the things that don't have good net and doing more with the things that do have good net the corollary to that is growth versus net that's the J. Peterman story is all the sudden we're going to try and grow this company and we're going to grow it in four different directions and we're going to grow it in areas we've never been before and and I killed him I was involved with a very interesting business some years ago we had if you ever been to my office not that it's anything you know to put on your tour of Phoenix or anything to see but if you've ever been to my office well my theory is money in an office is money not in my pocket so I mean it's like a hovel but in this dump that is an odd there's a shelf full of eagles and we at one time were in the eagle business I mean live eagles it tastes like chicken I mean see if you got a really astute group one of the ways you can save time is just give it him the punchline you don't tell the joke you just the these e this eagle business we had a brass eagle some of you will remember maybe if it doesn't ring any other bells for you if you were success magazine readers third of a page vertical ad for the dare to be a winner eagle today if we were still in business today accessories would be putting us out of business but we sold hundreds of thousands of these brass birds but our customers were not consumed not like you buying it to put on your bookshelf our customers were like Boy Scouts of America buying a thousand birds at a whack for their awards program the US Navy buying thousands and birds corporation type clients buying them from incentives and awards that was a nifty little business very profitable at a certain level one gal ran the whole thing took all the customer calls processed all the orders ordered all the stuff made sure all stuff was your one employee one catalog a year printed catalog sent it out to the customers income New York everything we did to try and press that business larger lost money I mean everything we tried we did a lot of logical stuff too I mean we saw okay it's Eagles right they want stuff with eagles on it so we should be able to do polo shirts baseball caps engraved briefcases none of that so okay these are all sales executives buying from us that's like 80% of the business so let's sell them tapes so motivational stuff educational stuff nope they wanted to buy a brass bird on a wood block that's it and that's all that's all they were gonna buy from us now they had in their head these are the guys we get the brass bird on the wood block from and we were woefully unsuccessful at educating any of them that we did anything else a point being there are some businesses that you can't press beyond a certain point profitably some businesses are not made to just continue to grow and grow and grow and grow and when you try and grow them beyond their level of profitability they turn from winners into losers and so growth is not necessarily always a answer last thing I want to talk to you about we actually talked about this a little bit before but oh they're utter the answer question about the valuations of the internet business there are utter nonsense I mean if you're gonna start to if you're gonna try and tell me that Amazon comm is worth 30% of what general motors is worth you know we have just been smoking some funny weed all of that has happened because of the massive influx of rank amateur investors who have no worth the idea what they're doing and are easily excited and there's massive stock manipulation going on because of that you see even ordinary businesses are getting huge bumps in their stock if they just announced that they're going to do anything with the internet the most recent blatant manipulation that I thought was very interesting was the victorious secret thing that was done last week the fashion show on the net you all read about this they had what 10 million hits or something in one day and this this was alive so those of you didn't see the news story you went to the victorious secret website which is like new they only been up for a couple months and for 45 minutes or something they had a live fashion show which the best description I heard of the pick the actual picture image was like this big and you know it was like the best description I saw was at a Wall Street show they said it looked like old bad porno movies with better-looking women so but but here's here here's the point that thing didn't sell any lingerie and it wasn't intended to sell any lingerie there they're not that dumb what it was intended to do was kite the stock that's what that was all about and so there's no value there and in all probability there's not gonna be any value there to support like a company like Amazon and believe me I love Amazon and I hope long live Amazon because for authors they're wonderful but long-term play there's nothing there and they're buying market share in a place there is no share come in here by a fourth of the room you got something right go there by a fourth of the what do you buy it's unlimited you're buying a fourth of nothing because you can't quantify it so there's there's no value there it's nonsense and it's gonna stay nonsense yeah well they know that's brand-building advertising like the Budweiser frogs and there's market share there's a finite market share there to get there's a certain amount of beer sold every year every month where you compete for your share of that revenue there's a certain amount of shelf space that these companies compete for their advertising helps them get a store to agree to put cardboard frogs and the Budweiser logo at the cash register instead of putting the Miller logo there that it's not my kind of advertising but it is rational advertising in a particular environment this other stuff's completely irrational and in terms of having any value the VAT whatever values created today could be gone tomorrow it's even a greater illusion than a building equity value in a network marketing business for example where you don't control the distribution yeah I don't want to spend a lot of time on this so well if you want to be if you want to be strictly measurable then giving them a 20% growth rate for the next every year forever that roughly now they need two decades to make up for their losses so we should all live so long so you know so if you want to be harshly measurable in their quest to buy market share at any cost fueled by an unlimited amount of capital flowing into them from a bunch of nutcases they have they have created losses that are beyond anybody's level of comprehension and all of that is supposed to buy market share now what I say is you can't quantify the market share say because it's not again like the beer example there's so many supermarkets they got so many square footage shelf space there's so many places you can put beer there's reliable sales numbers from year to year so there's things that you can get a piece of and know what you got here there's no barrier to entry to competition okay like you know like this month for example there's a big is a little player that became a big player in the book business on the net it's it's mystery world dot com I think her mysteries world or something like that and they now have every mystery book ever written known to man available at their site well in their category they got four times the diversity of inventory that Amazon dot com does now if somebody starts carving up the business there's no way even Amazon will keep up with them and they came out of nowhere there's no barrier for their entry but they just took part of Amazon's business how much market share they each got who the hell knows so the value is a is an illusion that's not to say that doing I'm not saying doing business on the internet is an illusion that's not my position but what I'm saying is he asked about the equity value in the business it's not it's not there and you start to see some adjustments now we talked earlier about reading and in some cases this is preachy to the converted because you're here but you know I have a lot of clients that like they they stopped learning anything new about their business X number of years ago and here's my like question list form what do you know this week that you didn't know last week have you gained any information and if you haven't gained information someone who is gaining information will eventually take a big bite out of your butt the one thing that is true or that ever is things are changing fast here for linked marketing issues the first is I'm always befuddled I guess by small businesses we yes okay I am using a mic I'm fun no they did everything they did everything apparently that they can do I don't know why this is do we know why this is bad planning get echo I got you okay I understand I understand okay because we're not going to rearrange the whole room my answer is should have got here sooner got a front seat you know I'll do the best I can guys but the you're gonna get Jeff a while he may want to use a handheld which if you do then you'll make a few of these people happy who are unhappy at the moment here are four linked marketing issues the first is I started to say I'm kind of befuddled by small companies that are always in a hurry they tend to be initially entrepreneurially driven everybody knows who the leader is everybody thinks they're dealing with a person they tend to be in a mad rush to become to look like if not become to look like large nameless faceless corporate institutions when all of the evidence is that people prefer to deal with people not with nameless faceless institutions and the best example of all that I think is is what happened at Chrysler with I a coca it's certainly the principle of massive action helped and doing a number of things to save the company but one was something he would rather never have done and hated doing was TV commercials but they were vitally necessary to put a person's face on that company and to parade in front of the public somebody that they might trust in a case where they could not and would not trust the company and heading not done that I don't know that there'd be a Chrysler here today and it just seems to me that given the choice you want to do personality driven marketing not corporate driven marketing Dave Thomas is another great example Dave doing TV commercials with Dave is like agonizing he doesn't like it it takes him 50 takes to say hello and you know it's just a painful process but you know they tried everything else and nothing else works as well as him so I think given the choice you want to do personality driven marketing the second one celebrity really has two sides to it one is I continually say to people that I think they in many cases missed the boat by not linking celebrities real celebrities be it Hollywood or be it sports to their product services and their businesses I think this is a woefully underused opportunity and there's there's abundant evidence that it makes all the difference in the world you could not sell that stupid grill without George Foreman or somebody like him home shopping has discovered nine out of ten products you better have a famous person or they don't work exceptions are like round-papils products where the product really is the star small businesses local businesses get bumps when they do celebrity marketing be they local celebrities or national celebrities what's important for you know about this is it's not as expensive as everybody thinks it's not all that difficult to do there are more of them looking for a paycheck than you would ever possibly imagine and when particularly when you're doing print and you're doing regional or local not national campaigns there are lots and lots and lots of folks available in the five to fifteen thousand dollar a year range for like unlimited use of picture endorsement autograph and then you can get more complex from there the other edge of this is by all means make yourself a celebrity in your clientele and in your target market and by that I mean do the things that celebrities do right right a book that's why do interviews get articles published be visible at major events within the industry be seen with other famous people do all of the things that a celebrity does in order to be a celebrity and there are in almost every industry in every target group there are what I call and I include myself in a group famous people that nobody ever heard of you know we're in an airport we're not famous but it within our clientele we are famous and you probably don't care and you may not even want to be so famous that you can't walk through an airport but it's nice to be so famous that you can't walk through a property and casualty insurance convention without being mobbed by people who want to talk to you that's good business and and that doesn't happen by accident you create it this credibility believability thing is an ever increasingly important distinction to make and you have to do both but the credibility thing is less and less effective it's just mandatory yes you have to you have to demonstrate that you've been in business for some period of time depending on the industry you have to demonstrate that you belong to certain associations it's useful if you've been in business since 1903 if you have you want to say it if you serve 24 billion hamburgers you want to say I've served 24 billion hamburgers but all that by itself is no longer highly persuasive in the marketplace what's more important in the marketplace is that they can identify with you or your testimonials in some way that they can believe you let me give you an example in the in the network marketing industry one of the client companies I've worked with in recent years a company owned by Rexall and Rexall you all remember if you're my age or older the neighborhood drugstore with a little orange and blue sign and if you go back far enough soda fowls and at one time Rexall well first of all they were actually the first company they invented franchising in the United States and at one time they were the dominant player in the drugstore business they obviously are no longer that today but they have enormous brand identity and with an age demographic of 40 plus the name has enormous credibility but what's persuasive for them in recruiting people into their business is all of the testimonials from doctors and nurses and healthcare professionals about how fantastic the products are and about how fantastic the business opportunity is the credibility alone would not build their business the believability of these people with whom other people can identify saying here's this very specific detail how this has worked for me is more important it by itself with no credibility might not build the business but the two linked together have enormous power and so you want both specialization logic tells you specialists make more money than generalists and we are we're increasingly a fragmented marketplace network marketing which has never been an industry prone to specialization it's happening company like Rexall is focused totally on recruiting doctors there's another such company we could totally almost exclusively focused on recruiting dentists into their business and so specialization is occurring everywhere in cable TV it hasn't happened nearly as fast as everybody predicted but it is happening you're starting to see stations like the food channel and the golf channel actually able to survive and support themselves on advertising and everybody's belief in the industry is that you will although nowhere near at the pace we thought we would you will see an increasing number of very specialized channels for people with very specialized interests I think it's important to appear to be a specialist even if you are not
